questionText: The finishing times, $X$ minutes, of runners in a marathon are modelled by $X \sim N(240, 30^2)$. The fastest $20\%$ of runners finish in under $t$ minutes. Find $t$, giving your answer to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and the condition
steps.explanation: Finishing times are normal with mean $\mu = 240$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 30$. The fastest runners have the shortest times, so ‘the fastest $20\%$ finish in under $t$’ means $P(X < t) = 0.20$.

steps.description: Recognise this as a lower-tail probability
steps.explanation: The fastest times are the smallest, and they lie to the left of $t$, so the condition is already an area to the left and the inverse-normal applies directly.
The standardised value with $20\%$ of the area to its left is $z = -0.8416$. It is negative because $t$ lies below the mean — only the quickest fifth of runners finish sooner.
Standardising the unknown time $t$ must give this $z$-value, so the two are set equal.
Multiplying both sides by $\sigma$ and adding $\mu$ makes $t$ the subject — the general rule for turning a $z$-value back into a finishing time.
Putting in the mean $240$, the standard deviation $30$ and the $z$-value $-0.8416$ gives the time in minutes.
The arithmetic gives a time of about $214.75$ minutes.

steps.description: State the answer to 3 significant figures
steps.explanation: Rounding gives the time under which the fastest $20\%$ of runners finish.
The time is below the mean of $240$ minutes, as expected: the fastest runners are quicker than average, so their cut-off time must sit to the left of the mean.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $t \approx 215$ minutes (3 s.f.) — the time under which the fastest $20\%$ finish.
